---
title: "Emotikon: Comparing Original and Transformed Metrics"
engine: julia
julia:
  exeflags: ["--project", "--threads=auto"]
---

::: {.objectives}
**Learning objectives:** after working through this page you will be able to:

- compare models fit on the original versus the transformed response metric;
- assess whether a key interaction depends on the choice of response transformation.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Before you start"}
**Prerequisites:** [The Emotikon Project: Model Complexification and PCA](fggk21.qmd).

**Datasets used:** `fggk21` (Emotikon; see the [dataset catalog](../../getting-started/datasets.qmd)).
:::

In @Fuehner2021 the original metric of two tasks (Star, S20) is time, but they were transformed to speed scores in the publication prior to computing z-scores.
The critical result is the absence of evidence for the age x Sex x Test interaction.
Is this interaction significant if we analyze all tasks in their original metric?

Fitting the LMM of the publication takes time, roughly 1 hour.
However, if you save the model parameters (and other relevant information), you can restore the fitted model object very quickly.
The notebook also illustrates this procedure.

## Getting the packages and data

```{julia}
#| code-fold: true
using AlgebraOfGraphics
using Arrow
using CairoMakie
using DataFrames
using DataFrameMacros
using MixedModels
using MixedModelsMakie
using RCall
using SMLP2026: dataset, FITSDIR
using Serialization
using StatsBase
```

### Data and figure in publication

```{julia}
dat = DataFrame(dataset(:fggk21))
```

```{julia}
@transform!(dat, :a1 = :age - 8.5);
select!(groupby(dat, :Test), :, :score => zscore => :zScore);
describe(dat)
```

### Data and figure with z-scores based on original metric

## LMMs

### Contrasts

```{julia}
contrasts = Dict(
  :Test => SeqDiffCoding(),
  :Sex => HelmertCoding(),
);
```

### Formula

```{julia}
f1 = @formula zScore ~
  1 +
  Test * a1 * Sex +
  (1 + Test + a1 + Sex | School) +
  (1 + Test | Child) +
  zerocorr(1 + Test | Cohort);
```

### Restore LMM m1 from publication

  - Command for fitting LMM m1 = fit(MixedModel, f1, dat, contrasts=contr)
  - Fit statistics for LMM m1: Minimizing 5179  Time: 0 Time: 1:00:38 ( 0.70  s/it)

```{julia}
m1x = LinearMixedModel(f1, dat; contrasts)
restoreoptsum!(m1x, joinpath(FITSDIR, "fggk21_m1_optsum.json"))
```

```{julia}
VarCorr(m1x)
```

### Restore new LMM m1_om Star and S20 in original metric

  - Command for fitting LMM m1_om = fit(MixedModel, f1, dat_om, contrasts=contr)
  - Minimizing 10502 Time: 0 Time: 2:09:40 ( 0.74  s/it)
  - Store with: julia> saveoptsum(joinpath(FITSDIR, "fggk21_m1_om_optsum.json"), m1_om)
  - Only for short-term and when desperate: julia> serialize(joinpath(FITSDIR, "m1_om.jls"), m1_om);

#### ... restoreoptsum!()

```{julia}
#| eval: false
m1_om = LinearMixedModel(f1, dat; contrasts);
restoreoptsum!(m1_om, joinpath(FITSDIR, "fggk21_m1_om_optsum.json"));
```

#### ... deserialize()

```{julia}
#| eval: false
m1x_om = deserialize(joinpath(FITSDIR, "m1_om.jls"))
```

```{julia}
#| eval: false
VarCorr(m1x_om)
```

### Residual diagnostics for LMM m1

Residual plots for published LMM

```{julia}
#scatter(fitted(m1x), residuals(m1x)
```

```{julia}
#qqnorm(m1x)
```

### Residual diagnostics for LMM m1_om

Residual plots for LMM with Star and Speed in original metric.

```{julia}
#scatter(fitted(m1_om_v2), residuals(m1_om_v2)
```

```{julia}
#qqnorm(m1_om_v2)
```

::: {#refs}
:::

## Exercises

1. **Does the conclusion hold?** State, in one sentence, whether the age-by-sex-by-test interaction is significant when the two timed tasks are analyzed in their original (time) metric rather than transformed to speed. What does that imply about the robustness of the published conclusion?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
The page's analysis lets you check whether the absence of evidence for the three-way interaction survives using the original metric. If the conclusion is the same under both metrics, it is robust to the transformation; if it flips, the transformation is doing substantive work and must be justified on measurement grounds, not convenience.
:::

2. **Generalize the check.** What would you compare (and how) to decide whether the original or transformed metric gives a better-behaved model, setting aside the interaction question?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
Compare residual diagnostics (symmetry, homoscedasticity, normal quantile plots) between the two fits, since the metrics are not nested and cannot be compared by likelihood directly. The metric whose residuals better satisfy the model assumptions is preferable on statistical grounds.
:::

---

*This page was rendered from git revision {{< git-rev short=true >}} using Quarto {{< version >}} and Julia {{< julia-version >}}.*
